service_summary:
The Managed Detection & Response (MDR) services can be delivered through different technological types: Each Service will start with the Transition Service(s) to bring it into production which will start the Run Services. The transition Services consists of two (2) types: After the transition, the Managed Services will start, which consists of three (3) types: The following table consists of a high-level overview of the Transition & Run services of each of the services: | | Managed Detection & Response for Microsoft | | Type | ENDPOINT Defender for Endpoint | SIEM Microsoft Sentinel | IDENTITY Defender for Identity | CLOUD Defender for Cloud | MS XDR MS 365 Defender | OT Defender for OT | PHISHING Defender for Office365 | | DETECTION | Advanced Threat Intelligence | x | x | x | x | x | x | x | | Threat Hunting | | | | | 24x7 Security Monitoring | x | x | x | | RESPONSE | Threat Response | x | x | x | x | x | Advice Only | x | | Cyber Security Advisory | x | | PLATFORM | License Management | | | Configuration & Release management | x | x | x | x | x | x | x | | Health Monitoring | | User Management | | Log Management | | | | | | | | Custom use-case Development | | optional | | | | | | | TRANSITION | Technical Implementation | x | x | x | x | x | x | x | | Operational Readiness | x | x | x | x | x | x | x |
Managed Detection & Response for Microsoft Type ENDPOINTDefender for Endpoint SIEMMicrosoft Sentinel IDENTITYDefender for Identity CLOUDDefender for Cloud MS XDRMS 365 Defender OTDefender for OT PHISHINGDefender for Office365 DETECTION Advanced Threat Intelligence x x x x x x x Threat Hunting 24x7 Security Monitoring x x x RESPONSE Threat Response x x x x x Advice Only x Cyber Security Advisory x PLATFORM License Management Configuration & Release management x x x x x x x Health Monitoring User Management Log Management Custom use-case Development optional TRANSITION Technical Implementation x x x x x x x Operational Readiness x x x x x x x
key_features:
Transition Services consists of two (2) types of activities:
These two (2) activities form a consistent approach in completing the transition of the MDR Service towards Run Services. The start of the Transition Services begins when the contract is signed and ends when the “Ready for Service” requirements are met which are described below.
During the Transition Services Cegeka will not perform Security Alerts / Security Incident handling.

The goal of the technical implementation is to implement all technical measures as stated in the Statement of Work.
Cegeka will perform the following activities during the technical implementation:
For Cegeka to be able to effectively perform the technical implementation, the customer is responsible for:
The technical implementation will be formalized on a per project base in a Technical Design document. This document will include all the technical decisions related to the Service.

The goal of Operational Readiness is that both customer and Cegeka have a clear understanding of the governance and operational processes related to the Managed Detection & Response service.
Cegeka will perform the following activities during the Operational Readiness:
The Operational Readiness will be formalized on a per project basis in a Customer Scoping Document. This document will include all the operational agreements related to the Service.
The Customer is responsible for informing Cegeka of any changes which may impact the Service.

The table below reflect the responsibilities of Cegeka and Customer for the Cegeka Managed Detection & Response Services. | | Cegeka SOC | CUSTOMER | | | | | | DETECTION | | | | Advanced Threat Intelligence – Threat News update | R | I | | Advanced Threat Intelligence – IoC Matching | R | C | | Security Monitoring – Alert & Event Collection | R | C | | Security Monitoring – Triage | R | I | | Security Monitoring – In-Depth Investigation | R | C | | Security Monitoring – Customer Feedback | C | R | | Security Monitoring – Security Incident Notification | R | C | | Threat Hunting – Threat Hunt report | R | I | | Threat Hunting – Recurring Threat Hunts | R | I | | RESPONSE | | | | Threat Response – Pre-approved Containment actions | R | I | | Threat Response – Containment actions | R | C | | Threat Response – Containment approval & validation | I | R | | Threat Response – Follow Containment Advice | I | R | | Cyber Security Advisory – Service reporting | R | C | | Cyber Security Advisory – Service Meetings | R | C |
Cegeka SOC CUSTOMER DETECTION Advanced Threat Intelligence – Threat News update R I Advanced Threat Intelligence – IoC Matching R C Security Monitoring – Alert & Event Collection R C Security Monitoring – Triage R I Security Monitoring – In-Depth Investigation R C Security Monitoring – Customer Feedback C R Security Monitoring – Security Incident Notification R C Threat Hunting – Threat Hunt report R I Threat Hunting – Recurring Threat Hunts R I RESPONSE Threat Response – Pre-approved Containment actions R I Threat Response – Containment actions R C Threat Response – Containment approval & validation I R Threat Response – Follow Containment Advice I R Cyber Security Advisory – Service reporting R C Cyber Security Advisory – Service Meetings R C Responsible: The party who is responsible for the execution of the activity. Consulted: The party who must be consulted and must approve or provide input prior to execution. This party (partly) gives direction to the result. This is about two-way communication. Informed: That is the person/party who is informed about the decisions, progress and results achieved, so that the next step can be taken. This is about one-way communication. Not included in standard service and will be provided in Time and Material. ↑ Security Information Event Monitoring ↑

This is not the availability of the supported customers technologies. | | Calculation | | | Service Window | 24x7 | | Frequency | Per Month | | Objective (SLA) | ≥ 99.5% |
Definition The Availability is determined by the ability of Cegeka’s SOC platform to process new and existing security alerts/incidents. This is not the availability of the supported customers technologies. Calculation Service Window 24x7 Frequency Per Month Objective (SLA) ≥ 99.5%

The following definitions shall have the meanings hereby respectively assigned to them.
“Business Hours”: Monday through Friday from 8:00 to 18:00 CET/CEST excluding Belgian official holidays.
“Log Source” : device or system which generates Security Events
“Horizon” : Customer Portal provided by Cegeka for reporting and change management
“Initial Priority” : the priority which is pre-assigned automaticity to a Security Alert specific to a technology.
“SOC/CSIRT Analyst” : Member of the SOC performing Incident Response activities
“Security Event” : data recorded by a security system which represent security related occurrence, such as a failed or successful login attempt, allowed connection through a firewall, …
“Security Alert” :  A notification of a potential security anomaly violating security policies.​
“Security Incident” :  A Security Alert that has been triaged and has indications of malicious or unwanted activities which requires additional investigation.​
“Confirmed Security Incident” : A Security Incident that has been investigated and verified to include malicious or unwanted activities.​
“Service” : Managed Detection & Response services

The RFS indicates that all the prerequisites are completed during the transition period to start the Service. This is the moment Cegeka will perform the Managed Services on the full contracted scope and invoicing will start accordingly. The prerequisites for the RFS are: | Prerequisites | | ENDPOINT : at least one (1) EDR agent successfully installed on each of the OS in scope. | | SIEM : at least one (1) asset of each type of data sources onboarded | | IDENTITY : at least one (1) agent installed and functional | | CLOUD: at least one (1) workload protection active and functional | | MS XDR : at least one (1) EDR agent and (1) ITDR agent installed and functional | | OT : at least one (1) OT sensor installed and functional | | PHISHING: User reported phishing is functional | | Customer Scoping document is ready |
Prerequisites To reduce the operational pressure for the customer, a one-month learning period is applied after the start of the RFS date to fine-tune the environment to reduce the number of false positives. This implies that all Security Alerts will only be handled during Business Hours until the learning period has ended, and no SLAs are measured. After this period, the Service will go into full operational mode including all SLA. The RFS date has a maximum term of three (3) months after signing the contract unless explicitly agreed otherwise.

Cegeka works with a standardized “Security Incident Response Plan (S-IRP)” which describes how Cegeka handles information Security Incidents as part of the Managed Detection & Response Service to Customers.
Every occurrence of a Security Alert potentially resulting in a Security Incident is handled logically as described in this plan. The assignment of roles & responsibilities as well as the services and products available to Cegeka within a specific context will determine which parts of the plan will be executed by Cegeka, its customer or 3rd parties mandated by either or both.
This document describes the entire process, but the contractual agreements and Security Customer Scoping Document (S-CSD) will determine which of the process phases and activities will be handled by Cegeka under which conditions.
High-level representation of the S-IRP
The S-IRP consist of the following steps:
Included in Technical Implementation & Operational Readiness
Included in Advanced Threat Intelligence, Threat Hunting & 24x7 Security Monitoring
Included in Threat Response
Only follow up and support are part of this service.
Only follow up and support are part of this service.
Included in Cyber Security Advisory

Our SOC platform incorporated Advanced Threat Intelligence feeds by default. This helps improve real-time detection of the latest emerging threats and a more effective investigation of Security Alerts/Incidents.
Based upon the nature and type of threat, the following activities will be done to improve detection:
Based upon the nature and type of Security Alerts/Incidents, the following enrichments will be done:

On a monthly basis a threat hunter will proactively search through Security Events to find suspicious, or risky activities that have evaded detection by existing tools. Threat hunting will also consider historical data when searching for anomalies/outliers.
Cegeka will perform the following activities regarding Threat Hunting:

The goal of Health Monitoring is to continuously monitor the health of the technology used within the Managed Detection & Response service.
Cegeka is responsible for the following activities regarding Health Monitoring:
For Cegeka to able to effectively perform Health Monitoring, the customer is responsible for:

The priority of each Security Incident will be determined according to urgency and impact of the Security Incident. Based upon those two factors, the appropriated priority will be assigned accordingly: | | URGENCY | | IMPACT | | | L | M | H | | | | Users able to work (via workaround) No risk of external visibility No indications of cyber attack | Users partially able to work. Potential external visibility Indications of possible cyber attack | Users unable to work (no workaround) External visibility (actual or unavoidable) Clear indications of cyber attack | | L | 1 to 5 persons involved. No critical business services involved. No critical business info involved | P4 (Low) | P4 (Low) | P3 (Medium) | | M | Division/team or key personnel involved. Critical business services with reduced capacity/functionality Critical business info involved and possibly at risk | P4 (Low) | P3 (Medium) | P2 (High) | | H | Complete organization or building involved. Critical business services unavailable or compromised. Critical business info compromised (unauthorized access) | P3 (Medium) | P2 (High) | P1 (Critical) |
URGENCY IMPACT L M H L P4 (Low) P4 (Low) P3 (Medium) M P4 (Low) P3 (Medium) P2 (High) H P3 (Medium) P2 (High) P1 (Critical) The priority can change during the Security Incident Response Process.

Each Security Incident will be classified with an Area to represent the type of Security Incident which best suits the events seen during our investigations. Cegeka uses 17 standardized Security Incident Areas to classify Security Incidents: | Databreach | | Definition | Accidental or purposeful disclosure of information related involving personal data | | Examples | Confidential documents containing contact details are left unattended Confidential information containing customer user accounts is accessed by unauthorized individuals Confidential information containing employee data has leaked outside of the organization |
Databreach Definition Accidental or purposeful disclosure of information related involving personal data Examples | Disclosure of information | | Definition | Accidental or purposeful disclosure of information NOT containing any personal data | | Examples | Confidential documents are left unattended. Confidential information is accessed by unauthorized individuals Confidential information has leaked outside of the organization |
Disclosure of information Definition Accidental or purposeful disclosure of information NOT containing any personal data Examples | Loss or theft of equipment | | Definition | Loss or theft of any device/medium that contains Customer-related data | | Examples | Loss of laptop Theft of storage device Theft of hardcopy documents |
Loss or theft of equipment Definition Loss or theft of any device/medium that contains Customer-related data Examples | Reconnaissance | | Definition | Reconnaissance activities which have been observed on a device or the network. Reconnaissance activities are typically low-interaction and relying upon normal operation of a device/network and the information it discloses. Typically, no malicious payload is sent. | | Examples | Scanning of a website Port scanning of a server without any attempt to access the underlying services |
Reconnaissance Definition Reconnaissance activities which have been observed on a device or the network. Reconnaissance activities are typically low-interaction and relying upon normal operation of a device/network and the information it discloses. Typically, no malicious payload is sent. Examples | Malware | | Definition | Infection of a device with malicious software | | Examples | Ransomware message appears on screen Unusual behaviour after file download or website visit (slowness, unexpected moving mouse pointer, unexpected windows appearing, ...) |
Malware Definition Infection of a device with malicious software Examples | Technical intrusion | | Definition | (Possibility of) access to data or devices without authorization, possibly followed by malicious activities. This incident area is also used whenever a combination of more than one incident type is identified. | | Examples | Effective access to unauthorized device/application/database Unauthorized physical access to IT devices/locations |
Technical intrusion Definition (Possibility of) access to data or devices without authorization, possibly followed by malicious activities. This incident area is also used whenever a combination of more than one incident type is identified. Examples | Policy violation | | Definition | An end-user has performed an action that is against the Customer’s security policy | | Examples | Installation of unallowed/unwanted software (games, cryptominer, ...) Exhibition of illicit behaviour (inappropriate website visits, illegal downloads, ...) |
Policy violation Definition An end-user has performed an action that is against the Customer’s security policy Examples | Denial-Of-Service | | Definition | Purposeful disruption or degradation of IT services | | Examples | Internet-facing website partially or fully unavailable VPN connection slow or impossible |
Denial-Of-Service Definition Purposeful disruption or degradation of IT services Examples | Vulnerability | | Definition | Detection of presence/exploitation of a known vulnerability | | Examples | Report of vulnerability present in the information system Vulnerability exploitation detected by device or end-user |
Vulnerability Definition Detection of presence/exploitation of a known vulnerability Examples | Social engineering | | Definition | Detection of (attempted) attacks on information systems by use of non-technical or low-technology means - such as lies, impersonation, tricks, bribes, blackmail, and threats. Used for each such detection except phishing, for which a separate incident area exists | | Examples | Phone calls with falsified impersonation to trick a user to click a link Phone call with falsified impersonation pressuring an employee to disclose/send information to the caller Chat request asking to execute an action for which the requester isn’t authorized |
Social engineering Definition Detection of (attempted) attacks on information systems by use of non-technical or low-technology means - such as lies, impersonation, tricks, bribes, blackmail, and threats. Used for each such detection except phishing, for which a separate incident area exists Examples | Probing | | Definition | Detection of attempts of sending actual payloads to a system with the objective to learn something about the system or exploit the system. Probing often follows initial reconnaissance. | | Examples | Requests to port 1433 to detect the presence of SQL server and gather details about the SQL Server version, configuration and database service components Web application scans trying to detect specific vulnerabilities on an application |
Probing Definition Detection of attempts of sending actual payloads to a system with the objective to learn something about the system or exploit the system. Probing often follows initial reconnaissance. Examples | Brute forcing | | Definition | Detection of activities using a cryptanalysis technique or other kind of attack method involving an exhaustive procedure that tries all possibilities, one-by-one. | | Examples | Multiple and high frequency authentication attempts to access a server with a built-in account |
Brute forcing Definition Detection of activities using a cryptanalysis technique or other kind of attack method involving an exhaustive procedure that tries all possibilities, one-by-one. Examples | Compliance | | Definition | Detection of a technical non-compliance with security baselines | | Examples | The use of a built-in administrator account which should be disabled/locked Interruption of log forwarding towards SIEM |
Compliance Definition Detection of a technical non-compliance with security baselines Examples | Phishing | | Definition | Report of e-mail with malicious content | | Examples | E-mail with malicious file attachment E-mail with malicious hyperlink E-mail with fraudulent content |
Phishing Definition Report of e-mail with malicious content Examples | Identity theft | | Definition | Detection of events indicating someone wrongfully obtains and uses another person’s personal data in some way that involves fraud or deception | | Examples | Detection of successful simultaneous logons with a personal account from different locations |
Identity theft Definition Detection of events indicating someone wrongfully obtains and uses another person’s personal data in some way that involves fraud or deception Examples | SOC | | Definition | A yet to be categorized security incident to be handled by Cegeka SOC. This incident area is used for monitoring alerts on security related services. Tickets should never be closed with this area selected. | | Examples | N/A |
SOC Definition A yet to be categorized security incident to be handled by Cegeka SOC. This incident area is used for monitoring alerts on security related services. Tickets should never be closed with this area selected. Examples N/A | Other | | Definition | A security incident that does not fit into any of the other Security Incident Area | | Examples | N/A – all known Security Incident Area should fit into one of the other areas at the time of writing |
Other Definition A security incident that does not fit into any of the other Security Incident Area Examples N/A – all known Security Incident Area should fit into one of the other areas at the time of writing The Security Incident Area can change during the Security Incident Response Process.

Once a Security Incident has been identified, the customer will receive information about the Security Incident in the following ways based upon the priority: | | P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 | | Initial Notification | | By Telephone | x | x | | | | By Mail | x | x | x | | | By Portal | x | x | x | x | | Security Incident Updates | | Frequency | Per hour | Per 4 Business Hours | Per Business Day | | | Per mail | x | x | x | | | Post Incident Activities | | Security Incident Report | x | upon request [1] | | | | Security Incident Documentation | x | x | x | x | | Follow up actions | x | x | x | x |
P1 P2 P3 P4 Initial Notification By Telephone x x By Mail x x x By Portal x x x x Security Incident Updates Frequency Per hour Per 4 Business Hours Per Business Day Per mail x x x Post Incident Activities Security Incident Report x upon request[1] Security Incident Documentation x x x x Follow up actions x x x x The Initial Notification will be done once the Security Incident Ticket has been created and will be communicated towards the Operational Contact(s) which is defined in the Customer Scoping document. The Security Incident Updates are pro-active notifications from our SOC given to the customer on a recurring base depending on the priority of the Security Incident. These notifications will start once a Security Incident Ticket has been created and will stop when a containment action is performed, or advice is given.

The goal of Threat Response is to contain an active Security Incident as to reduce further impact in the customer’s environment. A threat Response only includes containment actions and does not include any activities related to eradicating malicious files or recovering impacted systems. Containment actions must always be approved by the customer before Cegeka can execute the recommended actions. The approval can either be “pre-approved” or ad-hoc. Based upon the nature of the Security Incident, the following response action(s) can be executed in the customer’s environment. | Action | Description | | REQUIREMENTS | | Host Responses | | Isolate Host | Restricting network access to and from the host using an EDR platform | MS Defender for Endpoint MS Defender for Server | | Terminate Process | Terminate Process on the host using an EDR platform | MS Defender for Endpoint MS Defender for Server | | Quarantine file on host | Quarantine file on the host using the EDR platform | MS Defender for Endpoint MS Defender for Server | | Isolate Pod on host | Restricting network access to and from the pod using an EDR platform | MS Defender for Containers | | Terminate Pod on host | Terminate POD on the host using an EDR platform | MS Defender for Containers | | Identity Responses | | Reset user password | This will prompt the user to change their password on the next logon, ensuring that this account can't be used for further impersonation attempts | MS Defender for Identity | | Disable user in Active Directory | This will temporarily prevent an AD user from signing in to the on-premises network | MS Defender for Identity | | Suspend user in Entra ID | This will temporarily prevent an Entra ID user from signing in. | MS Defender for Identity | | Clear session cookie in Entra ID | Clear a session cookie of an Entra ID user | MS Defender for Identity | | Soft Delete Mail | Soft delete mails from a user’s mailbox by moving in into the “deletions” folder | MS Defender for Office | | Confirm User Compromised | Marking the user as ‘high risk’ activating the Risky user policy. The user is prompted to securely change their password. | MS Defender for Identity | | Indicator Responses | | Block Process | Block PROCESS in environment | MS Defender for Endpoint MS Defender for Server | | Block Hash | Block HASH in environment | MS Defender for Endpoint MS Defender for Server | | Block IP | Block IP in environment | MS Defender for Endpoint MS Defender for Server | | Block Domain / URL | Block domain / URL in environment | MS Defender for Endpoint MS Defender for Server MS Defender for Office | | Block OAuth App | Block access to unauthorized OAuth app | MS Defender for Cloud Apps |
Action Description REQUIREMENTS Host Responses Isolate Host Restricting network access to and from the host using an EDR platform MS Defender for Endpoint MS Defender for Server Terminate Process Terminate Process on the host using an EDR platform MS Defender for Endpoint MS Defender for Server Quarantine file on host Quarantine file on the host using the EDR platform MS Defender for Endpoint MS Defender for Server Isolate Pod on host Restricting network access to and from the pod using an EDR platform MS Defender for Containers Terminate Pod on host Terminate POD on the host using an EDR platform MS Defender for Containers Identity Responses Reset user password This will prompt the user to change their password on the next logon, ensuring that this account can't be used for further impersonation attempts MS Defender for Identity Disable user in Active Directory This will temporarily prevent an AD user from signing in to the on-premises network MS Defender for Identity Suspend user in Entra ID This will temporarily prevent an Entra ID user from signing in. MS Defender for Identity Clear session cookie in Entra ID Clear a session cookie of an Entra ID user MS Defender for Identity Soft Delete Mail Soft delete mails from a user’s mailbox by moving in into the “deletions” folder MS Defender for Office Confirm User Compromised Marking the user as ‘high risk’ activating the Risky user policy. The user is prompted to securely change their password. MS Defender for Identity Indicator Responses Block Process Block PROCESS in environment MS Defender for Endpoint MS Defender for Server Block Hash Block HASH in environment MS Defender for Endpoint MS Defender for Server Block IP Block IP in environment MS Defender for Endpoint MS Defender for Server Block Domain / URL Block domain / URL in environment MS Defender for Endpoint MS Defender for Server MS Defender for Office Block OAuth App Block access to unauthorized OAuth app MS Defender for Cloud Apps The pre-approved containment action(s) and relevant scope will be formalized in the Customer Scoping document.

Service meetings are standard included monthly unless explicitly mentioned otherwise. During monthly Service meetings, the Cyber Security Advisor will present security Service reporting.
Service Meetings will be held during Business hours unless explicitly agreed otherwise.

The Service Reporting contains all insights regarding the Managed Detection & Response Service during that month.
The following insights will be reported:

The Horizon platform is a central digital engagement point for customers which can be consulted via the user-friendly web interface.
The Horizon Portal can be used for the following activities:
This platform will continuously be improved and developed with more features.

The Cyber Security Advisor is the point of contact for all tactical and strategic security topics and acts as a liaison between Customer’s security stakeholders and Cegeka.
The Cyber Security Advisor has the following responsibilities:
The Cyber Security Advisory is delivered based on an agreed number of days per month, including monthly security Service meetings, all activities required to prepare the meetings and the actions related to decisions agreed upon during the meetings.
The licenses for the technologies are not part of the Service itself but can be provided by Cegeka if required.

The goal of Configuration & Release Management is to ensure the quality and performance of the technology used within the Managed Detection & Response service.
Cegeka is responsible for the following activities regarding Configuration & Release Management:

A SIEM[2] collects and securely stores the machine data generated from Log Source types such as network, endpoint, identity & access management, anti-malware, and other. The data can be collected using syslog, forwarding agents, API, or other types of integration with data sources and event collectors. Once data has been collected, it will be normalized and correlated against threat detection rules which trigger Security Alerts.
Cegeka is responsible for the following activities regarding Log Management:
In case of a Managed Log Collector, Cegeka is responsible for the additional activities regarding Log Management:
For Cegeka to able to effectively perform Log Management, the customer is responsible for:
The onboarding of new data source types which are not in scope, requires an initial investigation and a separate implementation project on a T&M basis.

A use case indicates a customer-specific scenario based upon several technical events for which the customer would like to be covered within the Service. For each of these use cases, the following is defined and documented:
If Customer requires any of these additional use-cases, they can be developed on a T&M basis.

| TYPE | SCOPE managed by CEGEKA | | Cloud Platform owned by Cegeka | all users | | Cloud Platform owned by Customer | Cegeka users | | On-prem platform hosted by Cegeka | all users | | On-prem platform hosted by Customer | Cegeka users |
TYPE SCOPE managed by CEGEKA Cloud Platform owned by Cegeka all users Cloud Platform owned by Customer Cegeka users On-prem platform hosted by Cegeka all users On-prem platform hosted by Customer Cegeka users

For security reasons, only people identified as Authorized Contact are allowed to collaborate with our Cegeka SOC. Only they can interact with the Cegeka SOC. All other persons will automatically be prevented and will be notified towards the Customer. These are also the people who will receive the monthly report.
The Authorized Contacts will be formalized in the Customer Scoping document.

Any new Security Incident will result in a notification towards the customer Operational Contact(s).
If any blocking issues prevents Cegeka from continuing the Service as agreed, an escalation towards the customer Escalation Contact will be done.
The Operational & Escalation contact(s) will be formalized in the Customer Scoping document.

This Service Level is applicable to all technologies. | | | | Definition | The amount of time measured from the time a Security Alert is created in our SOC platform to the time a SOC analyst is assigned and Triage will start. | | Calculation | T0 : the time a Security Alert is generated in our SOC platform. T1 : the time a SOC analyst is assigned to perform Triage. | | Assumptions | Priorities can change during investigations | | Frequency | Per Month | | Objective (SLA) | 90% | | P1 | 30 min | | P2 | 30 min | | P3 | 30 min during Business Hours | | P4 | N/A |
Definition The amount of time measured from the time a Security Alert is created in our SOC platform to the time a SOC analyst is assigned and Triage will start. Calculation T0: the time a Security Alert is generated in our SOC platform. T1: the time a SOC analyst is assigned to perform Triage. Assumptions Frequency Per Month Objective (SLA) 90% P1 30 min P2 30 min P3 30 min during Business Hours P4 N/A

This Service Level is applicable to all technologies. | | | | Definition | The amount of time measured from the time a Security Alert is created in our SOC platform to the time a Security Incident is created, and in-depth investigation will start. | | Calculation | T0 : the time a Security Alert is generated in our SOC platform T2 : the time a Security Incident is created, and in-depth investigation will start. | | Assumptions | Priorities can change during investigations. Only applies to true positive Security Incident | | Frequency | Per Month | | Objective (SLA) | 90% | | P1 | 60 min | | P2 | 2 hours | | P3 | 1 Business Days | | P4 | N/A |
Definition The amount of time measured from the time a Security Alert is created in our SOC platform to the time a Security Incident is created, and in-depth investigation will start. Calculation T0: the time a Security Alert is generated in our SOC platform T2: the time a Security Incident is created, and in-depth investigation will start. Assumptions Frequency Per Month Objective (SLA) 90% P1 60 min P2 2 hours P3 1 Business Days P4 N/A

This Service Level is applicable to all technologies. | | | | Definition | The amount of time measured from the time a Security Incident is created to the time when a first response is given. | | Calculation | T2 : the time a Security Incident is created. T3 : the time when a first response is given. | | Assumptions | Priorities can change during investigations. Only applies to true positive Security Incident(s) A first response can be either of these things: Feedback containing an in-depth investigation. Feedback containing a response advice. A request to execute a containment action. The execution of a pre-approved containment action | | Frequency | Per Month | | Objective (SLA) | 90% | | P1 | 90 min | | P2 | 4 hours | | P3 | 2 Business Days | | P4 | N/A |
Definition The amount of time measured from the time a Security Incident is created to the time when a first response is given. Calculation T2: the time a Security Incident is created. T3: the time when a first response is given. Assumptions Frequency Per Month Objective (SLA) 90% P1 90 min P2 4 hours P3 2 Business Days P4 N/A
